figures.caption: Fig. 1. Performance comparison on the TaxiBJ dataset. Bubble size denotes FLOPs. WaveSFNet achieves the lowest MSE with reduced complexity.
TABLE IV QUANTITATIVE COMPARISON OF DIFFERENT METHODS ON WEATHERBENCH SINGLE-VARIABLE TASKS, INCLUDING UV10, T2M, TCC, AND R.
TABLE V QUANTITATIVE COMPARISON OF DIFFERENT METHODS ON WEATHERBENCH MULTI-VARIABLE FORECASTING (R, T, U, AND V). ALL METRICS ARE AVERAGED OVER THE FOUR VARIABLES.

Fig. 2. Overall architecture and core modules of WaveSFNet. WaveSFNet follows an encoder–translator–decoder design. A wavelet-based multi-scale encoder extracts latent features from input frames. A TDI Block injects adjacent-frame differences, and Nt stacked ST Blocks apply spatial–frequency dual-domain gating after packing time into channels. A wavelet-symmetric decoder reconstructs predictions.
TABLE I DETAILED EXPERIMENTAL CONFIGURATIONS FOR DIFFERENT DATASETS. Cs DENOTES THE BASE CHANNEL WIDTH OF THE WAVELET CODEC, AND Ct DENOTES THE TIME-PACKED CHANNEL WIDTH USED BY ST BLOCKS.
TABLE III QUANTITATIVE COMPARISON OF DIFFERENT METHODS ON TAXIBJ.
TABLE II QUANTITATIVE COMPARISON OF DIFFERENT METHODS ON MOVING MNIST.
Fig. 3. Qualitative visualizations of WaveSFNet on TaxiBJ.
